measurements: gātra height = 2/7 upper storey pillar height
stūpi height = 1/7 upper storey pillar height
adhiṣṭhāna height = 0.1 pillar height (aedicula) or 1/7 upper storey pillar height
adhiṣṭhāna height = 1/2 aṅghri height - 1/6 aṅghri height
Pillar with capital height = 4x potikā height
Pillar without capital height = 3x potikā height
citrakhanda doucine = 0.5 mod, median band = 1 mod, 16-sided = 1 mod, top square = 2 mod
relationships: Pillars are structural components of pavilions (maṇḍapa) and various halls
Pillars rise from the adhiṣṭhāna (base) or plinth
Pillars are located below the uttara (architrave) and prastara (entablature)
Pillars can be installed above walls (bhitti) or foundation deposits
Pillars in vaiśya/śūdra buildings may be square, octagonal, circular, or 16-sided
Pillars are positioned in the middle of doors on upper storeys
Stambha-torana is placed in the space between pillars
Pillars can be named after their decorative motifs (e.g., elephants, lions)
Foundation deposits (garbhanyāsa) are placed under specific pillars (e.g., right of door, homastambha)
contextual_info: Pillar counts are strictly defined per building type (e.g., 4 for mallavasanta, 8 for pañcavasanta, 208 for garbha)
Pillar arrangement in halls should be adjusted according to circumstances
Specific woods are prescribed for different social classes and divine constructions
The fundamental building module (daṇḍa) is derived from the pillar's top width
Pillars in light buildings (laghu-gṛha) rise directly from the ground
Pillar counts in divine dwellings can be even or odd, but must be odd in human dwellings
